title: AI and Machine Learning Interview Question Bank
subtitle: Advanced question set covering model evaluation, retrieval, prompting, and production AI concerns.

questions.question: What is the difference between precision and recall?
questions.intent: Checks whether you understand two core classification metrics and their tradeoff.
options: A. Precision measures correctness of predicted positives, recall measures coverage of actual positives.
B. Precision is training speed, recall is inference speed.
C. They mean exactly the same thing.
questions.correct_answer: A. Precision measures correctness of predicted positives, recall measures coverage of actual positives.
questions.explanation: Precision answers how many predicted positives were actually correct. Recall answers how many real positives you successfully found. The right balance depends on the business cost of false positives versus false negatives.
compare: High precision: fewer false positives.
High recall: fewer false negatives.

questions.question: What is data leakage in machine learning?
questions.intent: Tests whether you can detect unrealistic evaluation setups.
options: A. Information from the future or target leaks into training and inflates metrics.
B. The dataset is stored in JSON.
C. The model file is too large.
questions.correct_answer: A. Information from the future or target leaks into training and inflates metrics.
questions.explanation: Leakage makes offline metrics look better than real-world performance because the model accidentally sees information it would not have at prediction time.
compare: Leakage present: misleading evaluation.
Leakage controlled: metrics are more trustworthy.

questions.question: Why use embeddings in semantic search?
questions.intent: Checks whether you understand meaning-based retrieval beyond keyword matching.
options: A. Embeddings map text into vectors so semantically similar items are closer together.
B. Embeddings are just compressed PDFs.
C. Embeddings remove the need for ranking.
questions.correct_answer: A. Embeddings map text into vectors so semantically similar items are closer together.
questions.explanation: Vector representations help retrieve related content even when the exact words differ, which is useful for question answering, recommendation, and RAG pipelines.
compare: Keyword search: exact or lexical matching.
Embedding search: meaning-oriented retrieval.

questions.question: What problem does Retrieval-Augmented Generation solve?
questions.intent: Tests whether you understand how external knowledge is injected into LLM answers.
options: A. It grounds generation with retrieved context instead of relying only on model memory.
B. It makes prompts unnecessary.
C. It guarantees truth in every answer.
questions.correct_answer: A. It grounds generation with retrieved context instead of relying only on model memory.
questions.explanation: RAG combines retrieval with generation so answers can use fresher, domain-specific documents. It improves relevance but still needs evaluation, ranking, and citation discipline.
compare: Pure LLM memory: simpler, but more limited and stale.
RAG: better grounding, but more pipeline complexity.

questions.question: Why is prompt templating useful in production AI systems?
questions.intent: Checks whether you separate prompt structure from application logic.
options: A. It makes prompts versionable, testable, and easier to update safely.
B. It eliminates the need for evaluation.
C. It trains the model automatically.
questions.correct_answer: A. It makes prompts versionable, testable, and easier to update safely.
questions.explanation: Treating prompts as structured assets improves maintainability. You can compare versions, run offline checks, and change instructions without burying long strings across the codebase.
questions.code_snippet: template = 'Summarize the text in 3 bullets:\n{text}'\nprompt = template.format(text=article)
compare: Prompt template: reusable and testable.
Inline ad hoc prompt strings: harder to govern.

questions.question: Which approach is usually better for batch inference throughput: sending one request per item or batching many items when the model and system support it?
options: A. Batching many items is often better for throughput when supported.
B. One request per item is always faster in all systems.
C. Batching only helps for SQL, not model serving.
questions.correct_answer: A. Batching many items is often better for throughput when supported.
questions.explanation: Batching can amortize compute and transport overhead, though it may increase single-request latency.
compare: Single-item calls: simpler, often lower throughput.
Batch inference: better throughput, sometimes higher latency.

questions.question: What is the trap in choosing a model based only on benchmark accuracy without considering latency or cost?
options: A. The 'best' offline metric model may be too slow or expensive for real product use.
B. Latency and cost are unrelated to AI systems.
C. Accuracy guarantees production success by itself.
questions.correct_answer: A. The 'best' offline metric model may be too slow or expensive for real product use.
questions.explanation: Real systems optimize across quality, cost, latency, and operational complexity rather than one metric alone.
questions.code_snippet: score = quality_weight * q - latency_weight * p95 - cost_weight * usd_per_1k
compare: Metric-only selection: narrow and risky.
Multi-objective selection: closer to production reality.

questions.question: Why is using future information in a feature pipeline a subtle but severe mistake?
options: A. It leaks information that would not exist at prediction time.
B. It only changes memory usage and nothing else.
C. Future information is acceptable if the model is large enough.
questions.correct_answer: A. It leaks information that would not exist at prediction time.
questions.explanation: Time leakage can make historical evaluation look unrealistically strong while real deployment performs much worse.
questions.code_snippet: # wrong: using post-event status to predict pre-event outcome
compare: Time-aware feature design: realistic evaluation.
Future leakage: inflated and misleading metrics.

questions.question: What is the trap in stuffing too many low-quality retrieved chunks into a prompt?
options: A. More context can dilute relevance and waste context window budget.
B. Low-quality chunks always improve truthfulness.
C. Models ignore irrelevant context automatically every time.
questions.correct_answer: A. More context can dilute relevance and waste context window budget.
questions.explanation: Better retrieval is often about ranking and selection quality, not blindly maximizing chunk count.
compare: Many weak chunks: noisy and expensive.
Fewer high-quality chunks: often better grounding.

questions.question: Why is prompt injection especially dangerous in tool-using AI systems?
questions.intent: Tests safety reasoning beyond plain text generation.
options: A. Malicious input may try to manipulate tool calls or override trusted instructions.
B. Injection only matters in SQL and never in prompts.
C. Models always perfectly distinguish trusted and untrusted text.
questions.correct_answer: A. Malicious input may try to manipulate tool calls or override trusted instructions.
questions.explanation: Tool-enabled systems raise the stakes because bad instructions can lead to harmful actions, not just wrong text.
compare: Plain text-only failure: bad answer risk.
Tool-using failure: action risk and stronger safety needs.

questions.question: Which is often the first optimization target in a slow embedding retrieval pipeline: micro-optimizing Python loops or measuring embedding calls, vector search latency, and reranking cost?
options: A. Measure the major stages first.
B. Micro-optimize loop syntax first.
C. Rename variables first because names affect runtime most.
questions.correct_answer: A. Measure the major stages first.
questions.explanation: In AI pipelines the bottleneck is often model inference, network overhead, search, or reranking rather than small Python syntax choices.
compare: Measured optimization: higher confidence and impact.
Blind micro-optimization: often low-value.

questions.question: What is a tricky failure mode of evaluating a chatbot only on curated ideal examples?
options: A. The model may look strong on clean demos but fail on messy real queries.
B. Curated examples are always stricter than production.
C. Real user data is never needed for evaluation.
questions.correct_answer: A. The model may look strong on clean demos but fail on messy real queries.
questions.explanation: Evaluation sets should reflect actual user behavior, ambiguity, noise, and adversarial cases where possible.
compare: Curated-only eval: optimistic picture.
Representative eval: more trustworthy product signal.

questions.question: Why is cosine similarity often used in embedding retrieval?
options: A. It compares vector orientation and is useful for semantic similarity.
B. It sorts documents alphabetically.
C. It replaces the need for indexing.
questions.correct_answer: A. It compares vector orientation and is useful for semantic similarity.
questions.explanation: Cosine similarity is a common way to compare embedding direction rather than raw magnitude.
compare: Cosine similarity: common semantic comparison metric.
No similarity metric: no ranking basis.

questions.question: Why is caching LLM outputs by raw prompt text alone sometimes a trap?
options: A. Model version, system prompt, temperature, or tool context may change output semantics.
B. Prompt text is always sufficient for all cache correctness.
C. LLM outputs should never be cached under any condition.
questions.correct_answer: A. Model version, system prompt, temperature, or tool context may change output semantics.
questions.explanation: Good cache keys reflect the real determinants of behavior, not just one visible string.
compare: Raw prompt-only cache key: simpler but risky.
Behavior-aware cache key: safer and more correct.
